title: Hello, World!
description: Write your first program in Go.

slides.title: The Anatomy of a Go Program 🦴
slides.content:
## Every Go Journey Begins Here

Let's break down the world's most famous program step-by-step.

Every Go program is made up of **Packages**.
A package is just a way to group related code together.

To make a program runnable, it **must** live in a package named `main`.

quizData.question: What must the top package declaration be for a program to be executable (runnable)?
quizData.explanation: Go specifically looks for `package main` to know it's a program that can be run, not just a library.

slides.title: See It In Action 💻

quizData.question: Where does a Go program start executing its code?

options.text: At the very top of the file

quizData.explanation: The `func main()` is the magic entry point for every Go application.

slides.title: Imports 📦
slides.content:
## Borrowing Code

We usually need code that other people wrote (like code to print to the screen). We bring that code into our program using the `import` keyword.

In our *Hello World* example, we used:
`import "fmt"`

This tells Go: "Hey, bring in the 'formatting' package so I can print things!".

quizData.question: Which keyword is used to bring other packages into your program?
quizData.explanation: Go uses the `import` keyword followed by the package name in quotes.
